name: JavaScript
description: The language of the web. Build interactive websites, front-end apps, and back-end services with Node.js.

lessons.title: Hello World & console.log
lessons.content: JavaScript runs in the browser and in Node.js. The console.log() function prints output to the console. It is the most basic debugging and output tool in JavaScript.

exercises.question: Print the message 'I am learning JavaScript' to the console.
hints: Use console.log() with the text in quotes

exercises.question: Which function is used to print output in JavaScript?
hints: It is part of the console object

lessons.title: Variables: var, let, const
lessons.content: JavaScript has three ways to declare variables. 'var' is function-scoped (old style). 'let' is block-scoped and can be reassigned. 'const' is block-scoped and cannot be reassigned. Prefer const by default, use let when you need to reassign.

exercises.question: Which keyword declares a variable that cannot be reassigned?
Declare a const called 'country' with value 'Morocco' and log it.

lessons.title: Functions & Arrow Functions
lessons.content: Functions are reusable blocks of code. Declare them with the 'function' keyword or as arrow functions (=>). Arrow functions are shorter and commonly used in modern JavaScript.

exercises.question: What is the correct arrow function syntax to multiply two numbers?
hints: Arrow functions use => after the parameters

lessons.title: Arrays & Array Methods
lessons.content: Arrays store ordered lists of values. Key methods: push() adds an item, pop() removes the last, map() transforms each element, filter() selects elements matching a condition, reduce() accumulates a value.

exercises.question: Which array method creates a new array by transforming each element?
hints: It maps each element to a new value

lessons.title: Objects & Destructuring
lessons.content: Objects store key-value pairs. Destructuring lets you unpack properties into variables in a concise way. The spread operator (...) copies object properties.

exercises.question: What does const { name } = person; do?
options: Extracts the name property into a variable
exercises.answer: Extracts the name property into a variable

lessons.title: Promises & Async/Await
lessons.content: Asynchronous code doesn't block execution. Promises represent future values. async/await is syntactic sugar over Promises that makes async code look synchronous and is much easier to read.

exercises.question: What keyword pauses async function execution until a Promise resolves?
hints: It can only be used inside an async function
